examples.text: hascine propter rēs maledicās fāmās ferunt.
examples.translation: Is it on account of these things that they spread slanderous reports?
examples.ref: c. 195 BCE, Plautus, Trinummus 1.2.149

examples.translation: “Oenotrian men tilled [the land]; now rumor [has it that their] descendants call the nation ‘Italy’ after the name of its leader, [Italus].”
examples.ref: 29 BCE – 19 BCE, Vergilius, Aeneis 1.532–533

examples.translation: To abandon one whose reputation is attacked.

examples.text: Fāma tamen clāra est; et adhūc sine crīmine vīxī.
examples.translation: My good name is nevertheless unstained; and so far I have lived without blame.
examples.ref: 43 BCE – c. 17 CE, Ovidius, Heroides 17.17, (translation Benham's Book of Quotations 1948)

examples.translation: Many fear their reputation, few their conscience.
examples.ref: 61 CE – c. 112 CE, Plinius Minor, Epistulae 3.20.9

noun.etymology_text: From Proto-Italic *fāmā, from Proto-Indo-European *bʰéh₂meh₂, from *bʰeh₂- (“to speak”). Cognate to Ancient Greek φήμη (phḗmē, “talk”).

examples.text: “… Date volnera lymphīs / abluam, et, extrēmus sī quis super hālitus errat, / ōre legam.” …
examples.translation: “Grant that I may wash her wounds with water, and, if any last breath wanders over her, I will gather it with my lips.”
examples.ref: 29 BCE – 19 BCE, Vergilius, Aeneis 4.683-685

examples.text: […] dum ne quem militem legeret ex eo numero quibus senatus missionem reditumque in patriam negasset ante belli finem.
examples.translation: […] provided he did not choose any soldier from those to whom the Senate had refused discharge and a return home before the end of the war
examples.ref: 27 BCE – 25 BCE, Titus Livius, Ab Urbe Condita 26.1

examples.translation: Read books.
Have you read this book?
It's Greek; it cannot be read.

verb.etymology_text:
Etymology tree
Proto-Indo-European *leǵ-
Proto-Indo-European *-eti
Proto-Indo-European *léǵeti
Proto-Italic *legōder.
Latin legō
From Proto-Italic *legō, from Proto-Indo-European *léǵeti, from *leǵ-. Cognates include Ancient Greek λέγω (légō, “to speak, to choose, to mean”) and Albanian mbledh. May be related to lēx.
For sense 6 (“to read”), compare typologically разбира́ть (razbirátʹ), разбира́ться (razbirátʹsja) (< брать (bratʹ)), разбо́рчивый (razbórčivyj) (по́черк (póčerk)).
verb.head_info_text: legō (present infinitive legere, perfect active lēgī, supine lēctum); third conjugation

noun.etymology_text: Borrowed from Ancient Greek λείριον (leírion). See there for more.
noun.head_info_text: līlium n (genitive līliī or līlī); second declension

examples.text: Eōsdem facile addūcam, ut tē haec quae vastāre iam prīdem studēs relinquentem, usque ad portās prōsequantur.
examples.translation: I shall easily persuade the same [men], that as you are leaving these [things] which you have long been eager to destroy, they may escort [you] all the way to the [city] gates.
examples.ref: 63 BCE, Cicero, Catiline Orations Oratio in Catilinam Prima in Senatu Habita.21

examples.translation:
from the beginning to the end
(literally, “from the egg to the apples”)
examples.ref: c. 35 BCE, Horatius, Sermones 1.3.6–7

examples.text:
, Latin Vulgate translation of Psalm 71:8; Canadian national motto
Ā marī usque ad mare.
examples.translation: From sea unto sea.

adverb.etymology_text:
From Proto-Italic *ū̆skʷe, from Proto-Indo-European *úds-kʷe, from *úd-s (“out, outward”, genitive) + *-kʷe (“and”). Cognate with Sanskrit उच्चा (uccā́), Younger Avestan 𐬎𐬯𐬗𐬀 (usca, “up, out”), Russian вы- (vy-, “out from”), Proto-Germanic *ūt, English out.
However, cf. Lewis & Short, which says: usquĕ, adv. [us- for ubs-, from ubi with locative s; and que for qued, old abl. of quis; v. Corss. Ausspr. 2, 471; 838; cf.: quisque, usquam].

examples.text: Quae rectis lineis suos ordines servant
examples.translation: Which preserve their order in straight lines
examples.ref: c. 52 BCE, Julius Caesar, Commentarii de Bello Gallico VII.23

examples.text: Via stultī rēcta in oculīs eius; quī autem sapiēns est audit cōnsilia.
examples.translation: The way of a fool is right in his own eyes: but he that is wise hearkeneth unto counsels. (Douay-Rheims trans., Challoner rev.: 1752 CE)
examples.ref: c. 347 CE – 420 CE, Hieronymus, Vulgate Proverbs.12.15

verb.etymology_text: Perfect passive participle of regō (“to keep or lead straight, to guide”). Inherited from Proto-Italic *rektos, corresponding to Proto-Indo-European *h₃reǵtós (“having moved in a straight line”), from Proto-Indo-European *h₃reǵ- (“to straighten, direct”).
verb.head_info_text: rēctus (feminine rēcta, neuter rēctum, comparative rēctior, superlative rēctissimus, adverb rēctē); first/second-declension participle

examples.translation: For God and for home (motto of Methodist Ladies' College, Melbourne)
Let the good fortune of the house stand (motto of Harrow School, England)

examples.text: nox erat et tōta lūmina nūlla domō
examples.translation: It was night, and [there were] no lights in the whole house.
examples.ref: 8 CE, Ovidius, Fasti 2.792

examples.translation: to drag into one's pocket
One is one's own creditor. (proverb)
to conceive on one's own

examples.text: bellī domīque; bellō domīque; vel bellī vel domī; domī bellōque; domī mīlitiaeque
examples.translation: in war and peace

examples.text: ut non quietior populus domi esset quam militiae
examples.translation: so that the people should not become lazier in the time of peace than that of war
examples.ref: 27 BCE – 25 BCE, Titus Livius, Ab Urbe Condita 1.38

noun.etymology_text:
For Proto-Italic *domos, from Proto-Indo-European *dṓm (“house, home”), from root *dem- (“to build”). Cognates include Ancient Greek δόμος (dómos), Albanian dhomë (“a chamber, a room”), Avestan 𐬛𐬀𐬨- (dam-) Sanskrit दम (dáma), Proto-Slavic *domъ and further to English timber. At least indirectly cognate to Latin dominus.
The feminine gender is probably due to the original root noun; attempts to transfer it to the 4th declension are due to 2nd declension feminines being unusual outside of tree names. Some manuscripts of Plautus show forms in dem-; De Vaan (2008) doubts their authenticity.
noun.head_info_text: domus f (genitive domūs or domī); irregular, variously declined, fourth declension, second declension

examples.text: Nihil agis, nihil moliris, nihil cogitas quod non ego non modo audiam sed etiam videam planeque sentiam.
examples.translation: You do nothing, you plan nothing, you think of nothing which I not only do not hear, but which I do not see and know every particular of.
examples.ref: 63 BCE, Cicero, Catiline Orations:

verb.head_info_text: cōgitō (present infinitive cōgitāre, perfect active cōgitāvī, supine cōgitātum); first conjugation

examples.text: omne adeo genvs in terris hominvmqve ferarvmqve et genvs æqvorevm pecvdes pictæqve volvcres in fvrias ignemqve rvvnt
examples.translation: So far does every species on earth of man and beast, whether the aquatic species, livestock, or painted-winged, collapse into the frenzies and the fire
examples.ref: c. 37 BCE – 30 BCE, Vergilius, Georgicon III

examples.translation: Poison is drunk from a gold cup
examples.ref: Seneca

examples.translation: sluggish under the shade
examples.ref: c. 41 BCE – 39 BCE, Vergilius, Eclogae 1.4

examples.text: Et Verbum caro factum est, et habitavit in nobis :
examples.translation: And the Word was made flesh, and dwelt among us,
examples.ref: Vulgate, Gospel of St. John 1:14

examples.text: Et ingressus angelus ad eam dixit : Ave gratia plena : Dominus tecum : benedicta tu in mulieribus.
examples.translation: And the angel being come in, said unto her: Hail, full of grace, the Lord is with thee: blessed art thou among women.
examples.ref: Vulgate, Gospel of St. Luke 1:28

examples.text: De introductione religionis Christianæ in Islandiam.
examples.translation: On the introduction of Christianity to Iceland.
examples.ref: 1774, Finnur Jónsson, Historia Ecclesiastica Islandiæ 1

examples.translation: The Gauls are rushing against the Romans.
The brother’s crimes against himself
examples.ref: 27 BCE – 25 BCE, Titus Livius, Ab Urbe Condita I.6

examples.translation: The Romans marched against their enemy
examples.ref: 27 BCE – 25 BCE, Titus Livius, Ab Urbe Condita I.12

examples.translation: and being distributed in tribes according to their respective nations
examples.ref: c. 52 BCE, Julius Caesar, Commentarii de Bello Gallico VII.19

preposition.etymology_text:
From earlier en, from Proto-Italic *en, from Proto-Indo-European *h₁én (“in”). Cognates include English in, Ancient Greek ἐν (en).
The ablative is from the locative, and the accusative is from the directional.

examples.translation: Marcus walks slowly to the tree.
